questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.stem: A triangle is divided by a single line from its apex to the midpoint of its base (figure). Count the total number of triangles in the figure.

figure.caption: Triangle with one median
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='40,120 160,120 100,30' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='100' y1='30' x2='100' y2='120' stroke='#475569'/><text x='35' y='140' font-size='8'>count the triangles</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Count small and combined triangles.

**Working.** Two small right triangles, plus the whole large triangle, give $2+1=3$.

**Answer.** $3$.

questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'Although the project was delayed, the team remained ____ about meeting the final deadline.'
questions.solution:
**Concept.** 'Although' sets up a contrast: despite a setback, a positive attitude persisted.

**Working.** The contrast with the delay calls for a positive word — 'optimistic'.

**Answer.** optimistic.

questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.stem: A is three times as old as B, and the sum of their ages is $48$ years. A's age is _____ years.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Express both ages in one variable.

**Working.** $A=3B$ and $A+B=48\Rightarrow 4B=48\Rightarrow B=12,\;A=36$.

**Answer.** $36$ years.

questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.stem: The square root of $196$ is _____ .
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Find the number whose square is $196$.

**Working.** $14^2 = 196$.

**Answer.** $14$.

questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.stem: A can finish a job in $12$ days and B in $6$ days. Working together, they finish it in _____ days.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Add the per-day work rates.

**Working.** $\dfrac{1}{12}+\dfrac{1}{6}=\dfrac{1}{12}+\dfrac{2}{12}=\dfrac{3}{12}=\dfrac{1}{4}$, so $4$ days.

**Answer.** $4$ days.

questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.stem: Using A=1, B=2, ..., Z=26, the sum of the letter values of the word 'COAL' is _____ .
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Add positional values of each letter.

**Working.** C$=3$, O$=15$, A$=1$, L$=12$; sum $=31$.

**Answer.** $31$.

questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.stem: A triangle has one line drawn from its apex to the midpoint of the base (shown). How many triangles are there in total?

figure.caption: A triangle with a single cevian from the apex to the midpoint of the base.
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 160 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img'><polygon points='80,20 20,110 140,110' fill='none' stroke='#334155' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='20' x2='80' y2='110' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='2'/></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Count the small triangles and the whole.

**Working.** The cevian makes $2$ small triangles, plus the original large triangle $=2+1=3$.

**Answer.** $3$.

questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.stem: How many triangles are there in the square with both diagonals drawn (shown)?

figure.caption: A square with both diagonals drawn, forming four triangular regions.
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 130 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img'><rect x='15' y='15' width='100' height='100' fill='none' stroke='#334155' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='15' y1='15' x2='115' y2='115' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='115' y1='15' x2='15' y2='115' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='2'/></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Count the small triangles and the larger ones formed by pairs.

**Working.** There are $4$ small triangles plus $4$ larger triangles (each half of the square), giving $8$.

**Answer.** $8$.

questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.stem: A bag has $4$ white and $6$ black balls. Two balls are drawn without replacement. The probability that both are white is _____ (round to two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Multiply conditional probabilities for draws without replacement.

**Working.** $\dfrac{4}{10}\times\dfrac{3}{9}=\dfrac{12}{90}=\dfrac{2}{15}\approx 0.13$.

**Answer.** $\approx 0.13$.

questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.stem: Find the next term of the series: $2, 6, 12, 20, 30, \,?$
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Look at the first differences.

**Working.** Differences are $4,6,8,10,\dots$ (increasing by $2$); next difference $=12$, so $30+12=42$.

**Answer.** $42$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: A binomial random variable has $n=5,\;p=0.2$. Its variance is
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Variance $=npq$ with $q=1-p$.

**Working.**
$$\sigma^{2}=5(0.2)(0.8)=0.8$$

**Answer.** $0.8$.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: In a balanced two-skip winder, the two empty skips and ropes balance each other. The net out-of-balance load due to a 10 t payload only, in kN, is ____ (two decimals). Take g = 9.81 m/s².
questions.solution:
**Concept.** With balanced conveyances the winder need only lift the payload imbalance.

**Working.**
$$F=10000\times9.81=98{,}100\ \text{N}=98.1\ \text{kN}$$

**Answer.** 98.1 kN.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: For an invertible $n\times n$ matrix $A$, which statements are TRUE?
options: The columns of $A$ are linearly independent
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Equivalent conditions for invertibility.

**Working.**
- $\det(A)\neq0$ — TRUE.
- Columns linearly independent — TRUE.
- $0$ an eigenvalue — FALSE (would force $\det=0$).
- Full rank $n$ — TRUE.

**Answer.** Options (a), (b) and (d).

questions.subject: Mine Ventilation
questions.stem: The oxygen content of normal fresh air is approximately _____ percent by volume.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Atmospheric composition.

**Working.** Fresh air contains about $20.9\%$ oxygen (and $\approx79\%$ nitrogen).

**Answer.** $\approx 20.9\%$.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Air-overpressure (airblast) from a blast is aggravated by
options: inadequate stemming, exposed detonating cord on the surface and too small a burden
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Airblast is a pressure pulse from venting gases.

**Reasoning.** Poor confinement — short stemming, surface detcord, small burden — lets gases escape to the atmosphere abruptly, generating air-overpressure (and noise). Good confinement minimises it.

**Answer.** Inadequate stemming/exposed detcord/small burden.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A theodolite is primarily used to measure
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The theodolite is an angle-measuring instrument.

**Reasoning.** It reads horizontal and vertical angles on graduated circles; with stadia or EDM it can also give distances, making it central to traversing and triangulation.

**Answer.** Horizontal and vertical angles.

questions.subject: Mine Ventilation
questions.stem: Because methane is lighter than air, it tends to
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Specific gravity of methane is about $0.55$.

**Working.** Being buoyant, it layers along the roof.

**Answer.** accumulate near the roof.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A luminous flux of 2000 lumens is spread uniformly over a working area of 50 m². The average illuminance, in lux, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Illuminance $=\dfrac{\text{luminous flux}}{\text{area}}$ (1 lux = 1 lm/m²).

**Working.**
$$E=\frac{2000}{50}=40\ \text{lux}$$

**Answer.** 40 lux.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Principal planes are defined as planes on which
options: the two normal stresses are equal
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Principal directions carry only normal stress.

**Working.** By definition the shear stress vanishes on principal planes.

**Answer.** the shear stress is zero.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Standard personal protective equipment for an underground coal miner includes
options: a safety helmet with cap lamp, self-rescuer, ear protection, dust respirator and safety boots
a flame safety lamp worn as a necklace
questions.solution:
**Concept.** PPE guards against multiple hazards.

**Reasoning.** The miner needs head protection and light (helmet + cap lamp), an escape breathing device (self-rescuer), hearing and respiratory protection, and protective footwear — addressing impact, gas, noise and dust risks.

**Answer.** Helmet/cap lamp, self-rescuer, ear and dust protection, boots.

questions.subject: Mine Ventilation
questions.stem: Methane is emitted at $0.1\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$ and must be diluted to a limit of $1.25\%$ by volume (figure). Compute the air quantity required ($\mathrm{m^3/s}$).

figure.caption: Methane dilution
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='40' width='160' height='45' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569'/><circle cx='70' cy='62' r='6' fill='#16a34a'/><line x1='90' y1='62' x2='170' y2='62' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#a)'/><defs><marker id='a' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#2563eb'/></marker></defs><text x='33' y='105' font-size='8'>CH4 0.1 m^3/s, limit 1.25%</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $Q=\dfrac{\text{gas rate}}{\text{limit fraction}}$.

**Working.**
$$Q=\frac{0.1}{0.0125}=8\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$$

**Answer.** $8\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Block A is $1000$ t at $2\%$ and block B is $3000$ t at $1\%$. The tonnage-weighted average grade is _____ percent.

questions.subject: Mine Ventilation
questions.stem: A small equivalent orifice for a mine indicates that the mine is
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A small orifice represents a large restriction.

**Working.** High resistance means the mine is hard to ventilate.

**Answer.** difficult to ventilate.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The principal hazard while sealing off (and later reopening) a fire area is
options: the formation of an explosive gas mixture as oxygen falls/rises through the explosive range
the cooling of the mine air
questions.solution:
**Concept.** During sealing/reopening the atmosphere passes through explosibility.

**Reasoning.** As oxygen is depleted (sealing) or admitted (reopening), the CH₄/air/inert mixture can transit the explosive zone in the presence of a hot fire — the most dangerous moment, managed using explosibility diagrams and remote sealing.

**Answer.** Risk of forming an explosive mixture.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: Two joint sets intersect to form a removable block that daylights in a rock slope, as shown. The most likely failure mode is:

figure.caption: Intersecting joints forming a block
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='40,120 160,120 100,40' fill='#d6d3d1' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='100' y1='40' x2='70' y2='120' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='40' x2='130' y2='120' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><text x='50' y='140' font-size='8'>two joints form a wedge</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Structurally controlled slope failures depend on discontinuity geometry.

**Reasoning.** When two discontinuities intersect and their line of intersection daylights in the slope face below the friction angle, the bounded block slides out as a wedge — wedge failure.

**Answer.** Wedge failure.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Which of the following are recognised ore-forming (mineral concentration) processes? (Select all that apply.)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Ore deposits form by physical and chemical concentration.

**Reasoning.** Magmatic segregation, hydrothermal precipitation and mechanical (placer) sorting all concentrate valuable minerals. Schistosity is a metamorphic fabric, not an ore-forming process.

**Answer.** Magmatic, hydrothermal, placer.

questions.subject: Mine Ventilation
questions.stem: For the same methane emission of $0.05\,\text{m}^3$/s, the air needed to hold the concentration to $0.5\%$ is _____ m$^3$/s.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $Q=\dfrac{q_{\text{gas}}}{C_{\text{allow}}}$.

**Working.**
$$Q=\frac{0.05}{0.005}=10.$$

**Answer.** $10\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A 'plan' differs from a 'map' chiefly in that a plan is drawn to a
options: small scale covering a large area
large scale covering a small area, with little distortion
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Scale distinguishes plans from maps.

**Reasoning.** A plan uses a large scale for a small area (e.g. a mine layout) showing fine detail; a map uses a small scale for a large area. Mine workings are shown on large-scale plans.

**Answer.** Large scale, small area.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: The choice of an underground mining method depends primarily on
options: the colour of the ore only
orebody geometry (size, shape, dip, depth), ground conditions, grade/value, and dilution/recovery economics
the distance to the nearest town
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Method selection is a multi-factor engineering decision.

**Reasoning.** Geometry sets which methods are geometrically feasible; ore and wall strength set the support strategy; grade/value and the dilution/recovery balance set the economics. Production rate and capital also matter.

**Answer.** Geometry, ground conditions, grade/value and dilution/recovery.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A pit must deliver 720 m³/h (bank). If each shovel gives an effective 240 m³/h, the number of shovels required is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Number of units $=\dfrac{\text{required output}}{\text{output per unit}}$.

**Working.**
$$N=\frac{720}{240}=3$$

**Answer.** 3 shovels.

questions.subject: Mine Ventilation
questions.stem: The single opening shown is used to characterise how 'tight' or 'free' a mine is to ventilate (figure). The equivalent orifice is:
options: the area of a sharp-edged orifice that would pass the same quantity under the same pressure as the whole mine
the total cross-sectional area of all airways added together
the area of the main fan drift
the perimeter of the largest airway

figure.caption: Equivalent orifice
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='30' width='140' height='60' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><circle cx='100' cy='60' r='14' fill='#ffffff' stroke='#475569'/><text x='34' y='108' font-size='8'>equivalent orifice of a mine</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Equivalent orifice expresses mine resistance as an aperture.

**Reasoning.** It is the area of an imaginary sharp-edged orifice that would pass the same air quantity at the same pressure difference as the entire mine; a large orifice means a free mine, a small one a tight mine.

**Answer.** The area of a sharp-edged orifice that would pass the same quantity under the same pressure as the whole mine.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Acid mine water at pH 3 is to be neutralised to pH 7. The increase in pH required, in pH units, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Change $=$ target pH $-$ initial pH.

**Working.**
$$\Delta\text{pH}=7-3=4$$

**Answer.** 4 pH units (achieved by adding lime/limestone).

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: The arithmetic mean of the data $2,\,4,\,6,\,8,\,10$ is _____ .
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Mean $=\dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$.

**Working.**
$$\bar{x}=\frac{2+4+6+8+10}{5}=\frac{30}{5}=6$$

**Answer.** $6$.

questions.subject: Mine Ventilation
questions.stem: Mine ventilation pressure is conventionally expressed in
options: pascals (Pa) or millimetres of water gauge
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Ventilation pressure is a static head.

**Working.** It is measured in Pa or mm of water gauge.

**Answer.** pascals or mm water gauge.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The 'incubation period' of a coal in relation to spontaneous combustion is
options: the time the explosion flame travels along the airway
the time from first exposure/oxidation of the coal to the onset of an active fire (or a defined temperature rise)
the burning time of a safety fuse
the life of a flame safety lamp battery
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Incubation period indexes proneness to heating.

**Reasoning.** It is the interval between exposure of coal to air and the development of an active fire; a short incubation period denotes a highly susceptible seam needing rapid extraction and sealing.

**Answer.** Time from exposure to onset of fire.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: The chief advantage of a gyro-theodolite for underground orientation is that it
options: determines true (geographic) north independently of the earth's magnetic field, so it is unaffected by steelwork
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A spinning gyro seeks the meridian.

**Reasoning.** It finds true north from the earth's rotation, free of magnetic disturbance from rails, supports and cables, giving azimuth underground without long correlation traverses.

**Answer.** True north, immune to magnetic interference.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The thermal (heat) environment experienced by an underground worker depends on
options: the combination of dry-bulb temperature, wet-bulb temperature (humidity), air velocity and radiant heat
only the depth of the mine
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Human heat balance involves several factors.

**Reasoning.** Comfort/heat stress is set by air temperature, humidity (which governs sweat evaporation), air movement (convective/evaporative cooling) and radiant heat from hot surfaces — not temperature alone.

**Answer.** Dry-bulb, wet-bulb, air velocity and radiant heat.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: An investment of $\textsf{Rs}\,10{,}000$ returns a single $\textsf{Rs}\,12{,}100$ two years later. The internal rate of return is _____ percent.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: Processing plus mining cost is Rs $24$ per tonne, the metal price is Rs $4000$ per tonne of metal and the recovery is $0.8$ (figure). Compute the break-even cut-off grade (%).

figure.caption: Value versus grade
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='30' x2='30' y2='110' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='200' y2='110' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='190' y2='40' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='110' y1='110' x2='110' y2='75' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='35' y='28' font-size='8'>value vs grade</text><text x='92' y='125' font-size='8'>cut-off?</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $g_c=\dfrac{\text{cost}}{\text{price}\times\text{recovery}}$, expressed as a percentage.

**Working.**
$$g_c=\frac{24}{4000\times0.8}=\frac{24}{3200}=0.0075=0.75\%$$

**Answer.** $0.75\%$.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: If the total cost of $10{,}000$ units is $\textsf{Rs}\,11{,}00{,}000$, the average cost per unit is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ .
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Unit cost $=TC/\text{units}$.

**Working.**
$$\frac{1100000}{10000}=110.$$

**Answer.** $\textsf{Rs}\,110$.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Square-set stoping is characterised by
options: a self-supporting open stope in strong rock
a complete interlocking timber framework supporting very weak ore and walls (high-grade, irregular, but costly)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Square-setting builds a structural skeleton as it mines.

**Reasoning.** Interlocking timber 'sets' fill the mined space, supporting extremely weak ground for high-grade, irregular orebodies. It is labour- and timber-intensive and now largely superseded by cut-and-fill.

**Answer.** Interlocking timber framework for very weak, high-grade ground.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A 5 m³ bucket digs with a fill factor of 0.9 (as shown). Compute the actual payload per pass (m³).

figure.caption: Bucket fill
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M50 40 L150 40 L135 95 L65 95 Z' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#b45309'/><path d='M58 70 L142 70 L135 95 L65 95 Z' fill='#a16207'/><text x='62' y='32' font-size='8'>capacity 5 m^3</text><text x='66' y='112' font-size='8'>fill factor 0.9</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Actual payload = rated capacity × fill factor.

**Working.**
$$V = 5 \times 0.9 = 4.5$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{4.5}$ m³ per pass.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: The engineering shear strain $\gamma$ relates to the tensorial shear strain $\varepsilon_{xy}$ by
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Engineering shear strain is twice the tensorial component.

**Working.** $\gamma_{xy}=2\varepsilon_{xy}$.

**Answer.** $\gamma = 2\varepsilon_{xy}$.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A wedge (V) cut consists of
options: parallel holes with an empty relief hole
pairs of holes drilled at an angle to meet, so a wedge-shaped prism of rock is blasted out to form the cut
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Angled-hole cuts wedge out a prism.

**Reasoning.** Opposing inclined holes meet near the back of the cut; firing them ejects a V-shaped wedge, opening the face. It needs a reasonably wide heading because of the angled drilling, and the pull is limited by face width.

**Answer.** Angled holes blasting out a wedge prism.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For plane (planar) sliding to be kinematically possible, the discontinuity must
options: daylight in the slope face and dip less steeply than the face but steeper than $\phi$
dip steeper than the slope face
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Daylighting and the friction condition govern feasibility.

**Working.** The plane must dip out of the face ($\psi_p<\psi_f$) and exceed friction ($\psi_p>\phi$).

**Answer.** daylight in the slope face and dip less steeply than the face but steeper than $\phi$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Under typical coal-mine regulations, work must stop and power be cut when the methane concentration in the general body of air at the face reaches about
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Statutory action limits keep CH₄ well below the explosive 5%.

**Reasoning.** A common rule withdraws persons / stops electrical equipment at about 1.25% CH₄ in the general body of air, with the return-airway limit lower (~0.75%), providing a wide safety margin below 5%.

**Answer.** About 1.25%.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: At the EOQ, ordering and holding costs are each $\textsf{Rs}\,1000$/year. The total variable inventory cost is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ .
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Total variable cost $=$ ordering $+$ holding.

**Working.**
$$1000+1000=2000.$$

**Answer.** $\textsf{Rs}\,2000$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Mine water discharged to surface streams commonly requires treatment to remove
options: suspended solids, acidity and heavy metals (e.g. via settling ponds and neutralisation)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Mine effluent carries solids, acidity and metals.

**Reasoning.** Sediment-laden, sometimes acidic and metal-bearing water is passed through settling ponds (to drop solids) and neutralisation (to raise pH and precipitate metals) before discharge to meet standards.

**Answer.** Suspended solids, acidity and heavy metals.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Annual demand $D=10{,}000$ units, ordering cost $S=\textsf{Rs}\,50$/order and holding cost $H=\textsf{Rs}\,4$/unit-year. The economic order quantity is _____ units.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\text{EOQ}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$.

**Working.**
$$\text{EOQ}=\sqrt{\frac{2(10000)(50)}{4}}=\sqrt{250000}=500.$$

**Answer.** $500$ units.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A GNSS/GPS receiver fixes its three-dimensional position by
options: trilateration from the ranges to at least four satellites of known position
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Satellite ranging by trilateration.

**Reasoning.** The receiver measures pseudo-ranges to several satellites; four are needed to solve for the three position coordinates plus the receiver clock error.

**Answer.** Trilateration from ≥4 satellites.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Compared with bord-and-pillar mining with standing pillars, surface subsidence over a longwall panel is
options: negligible and never reaches the surface
immediate and follows the face, forming a predictable subsidence trough
delayed by decades and always uncertain
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Total extraction with caving causes prompt subsidence.

**Reasoning.** As the roof caves behind a longwall face the overburden settles almost immediately, producing a travelling, predictable subsidence trough — unlike bord-and-pillar where standing pillars may delay or render subsidence uncertain.

**Answer.** Immediate, predictable subsidence trough following the face.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: For a fully loaded truck of gross weight 100 t with total resistance 70 kg/t (as shown), compute the rimpull (kg) required to keep it moving up the grade.

figure.caption: Loaded truck on grade
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='120' x2='220' y2='40' stroke='#92400e' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='120' y='52' width='34' height='18' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569' transform='rotate(-18 137 61)'/><text x='28' y='115' font-size='8'>GVW 100 t, total res 70 kg/t</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Required rimpull = total resistance per tonne × gross weight.

**Working.**
$$RP = 70 \times 100 = 7000$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{7000}$ kg.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A blast drills $100\ \text{m}$ of hole to break $1200\ \mathrm{m^3}$ of rock (figure). Compute the specific drilling ($\mathrm{m/m^3}$, three decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Specific drilling $=\dfrac{\text{metres drilled}}{\text{volume broken}}$.

**Working.**
$$\frac{100}{1200}=0.083\ \mathrm{m/m^3}$$

**Answer.** $0.083\ \mathrm{m/m^3}$.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: A project requires $\textsf{Rs}\,1{,}00{,}000$ now and returns $\textsf{Rs}\,30{,}000$ at the end of each year for $5$ years. At $10\%$, its net present value is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ . Round to the nearest rupee.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: A firedamp–air mixture detonates most violently at a methane concentration of about
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Maximum violence is near the stoichiometric mixture.

**Reasoning.** Complete combustion of methane needs roughly a 1:2 ratio with oxygen, corresponding to about 9.5–9.8% CH₄ in air; here the explosion pressure and flame temperature peak.

**Answer.** About 9.5–9.8%.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A triangle of height $9\,\text{m}$ rests on its base. The centroid is at a height of _____ m above the base.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The centroid is at $h/3$ above the base.

**Working.**
$$\bar y=\frac{9}{3}=3.$$

**Answer.** $3\,\text{m}$.

questions.subject: Mine Ventilation
questions.stem: A water-gauge reading of $50\,\text{mm}$ corresponds to _____ Pa (take $1\,\text{mm w.g.}=9.81\,\text{Pa}$).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $P=\text{mm w.g.}\times9.81$.

**Working.**
$$50\times9.81=490.5.$$

**Answer.** $490.5\,\text{Pa}$.

questions.subject: Mine Ventilation
questions.stem: The standard density of mine air used in ventilation calculations is approximately
questions.solution:
**Concept.** At normal temperature and pressure, air density is about $1.2\,\text{kg/m}^3$.

**Working.** This is the conventional standard value.

**Answer.** 1.2 kg/m$^3$.

questions.subject: Mine Ventilation
questions.stem: Blackdamp (stythe) in a mine is mainly
options: a mixture of carbon dioxide and nitrogen (oxygen-deficient air)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Blackdamp is the residual non-breathable air.

**Working.** It is rich in CO$_2$ and N$_2$ and low in O$_2$.

**Answer.** carbon dioxide and nitrogen.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Hydrogen sulphide (stinkdamp) in mines is characterised by
options: no smell and being lighter than air
a rotten-egg odour, high toxicity, and being heavier than air
questions.solution:
**Concept.** H₂S is a toxic, dense, malodorous gas.

**Reasoning.** It smells of rotten eggs at low concentration (but deadens the sense of smell at high concentration), is denser than air so collects low down, and is acutely toxic.

**Answer.** Rotten-egg smell, toxic, heavier than air.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: For $A=\begin{bmatrix}1 & 2\\ 3 & 4\end{bmatrix}$, the matrix $A^2-5A$ equals
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Cayley–Hamilton: a matrix satisfies its own characteristic equation $\lambda^2-(\operatorname{tr}A)\lambda+\det A=0$.

**Working.** Here $\operatorname{tr}A=5$ and $\det A=4-6=-2$, so
$$A^2-5A-2I=0\;\Rightarrow\;A^2-5A=2I$$

**Answer.** $2I$.

questions.subject: Mine Ventilation
questions.stem: An airway has the rectangular cross-section shown, 4 m wide by 3 m high. Its hydraulic mean radius ($m=A/O$), in m, is _____ (two decimals).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='40' y='40' width='120' height='80' fill='#e0f2fe' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><text x='100' y='138' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>width 4 m</text><text x='22' y='84' font-size='11'>3 m</text></svg>
figure.caption: Rectangular airway section

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $m=\dfrac{A}{O}$, $A=$ area, $O=$ perimeter.

**Working.**
$$A=4\times3=12\ \mathrm{m^2},\quad O=2(4+3)=14\ \text{m},\quad m=\frac{12}{14}=0.86\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $0.86$ m.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: For the decision branch shown, with a 0.6 probability of a ₹100 lakh gain and a 0.4 probability of a ₹50 lakh loss, compute the Expected Monetary Value (₹ lakh).

figure.caption: Decision tree branch
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='65' width='16' height='16' fill='#1f2937'/><line x1='36' y1='73' x2='120' y2='40' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='36' y1='73' x2='120' y2='110' stroke='#475569'/><circle cx='120' cy='40' r='5' fill='#fff' stroke='#475569'/><circle cx='120' cy='110' r='5' fill='#fff' stroke='#475569'/><text x='58' y='40' font-size='8'>p=0.6</text><text x='58' y='118' font-size='8'>p=0.4</text><text x='132' y='44' font-size='8'>+100</text><text x='132' y='114' font-size='8'>-50</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** EMV is the probability-weighted sum of outcomes.

**Working.**
$$EMV = 0.6(100) + 0.4(-50) = 60 - 20 = 40$$

**Answer.** EMV $= \mathbf{40}$ ₹ lakh.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: An ore block has plan area $200{,}000\ \mathrm{m^2}$, thickness $5\ \text{m}$ and specific gravity $2.5$. Compute the in-situ tonnage (t).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Tonnage $=$ area $\times$ thickness $\times$ density.

**Working.**
$$T=200000\times5\times2.5=2{,}500{,}000\ \text{t}$$

**Answer.** $2.5\times10^6$ t.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: For the 33-hole pattern shown (burden 3 m, spacing 4 m, bench height 10 m), compute the total in-situ rock volume broken (m³).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Volume = burden × spacing × height × number of holes.

**Working.**
$$V = 3 \times 4 \times 10 \times 33 = 3960$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{39600}$ m³.
